---
title: "Forcing Transitive Versions via Dependency Management"
tagline: "Or what to Do When a Transitive Dependency is the Wrong Version"
type: scenario
tool: maven
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When a Transitive Dependency is the Wrong Version*
This scenario demonstrates how to force a specific version of a transitive dependency, overriding what the intermediate libraries request.

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-b:1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibC[lib-c:1.0.0]
    Root -- forces --> LibC_Forced[lib-c:2.0.0]
```


```{.xml include="pom.xml" snippet="s6-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
"I said use 2.0.0, so use 2.0.0."

#### ! Actual
Using `<dependencyManagement>`, `lib-c` becomes `2.0.0`.

#### ! Reality
`lib-c-2.0.0.jar` is on the classpath.

#### ! Why
This scenario uses the exact same `<dependencyManagement>` mechanism as S3, but applies it to a *transitive* dependency instead of a direct one. There is no special "forcing" keyword in Maven; it's all handled by `<dependencyManagement>`. It works because Maven evaluates the `<dependencyManagement>` section after building the complete dependency graph but *before* selecting the final versions. This allows the managed versions to override any version chosen by the "nearest-wins" algorithm.

#### ! Fix
This is often the fix for "Nearest Wins" picking an old version, or to patch a vulnerability deep in the graph.

#### ! Risk

* LOW Forcing a version to align with a BOM or to patch a known vulnerability. This is a primary use case for this feature.
* MEDIUM Forcing a version to a newer version that has not been tested with the libraries that depend on it. This can lead to `NoSuchMethodError` if the new version has breaking changes.
* HIGH Forcing a version to an older version. This is very likely to cause problems, as the libraries that depend on it may be using features only available in the newer version.

#### ! Control
This is a safe and recommended pattern for enforcing policy.

#### ! Check

**Fast check (Maven):**

```bash
mvn dependency:tree
```

**What to look for:**
The version of `lib-c` in the tree. It will be `2.0.0`, overriding the `1.0.0` requested by `lib-b`.

**Conclusion:**
A version in `<dependencyManagement>` will override any transitive dependency version.

#### ! Scales
Strict constraints are vital for security compliance (e.g., "Must use OpenSSL >= 3.0").

#### ! Takeaway
`<dependencyManagement>` overrides transitive version resolution (nearest-wins), making it the primary tool for enforcing version policy across the dependency tree. Note that a direct version in `<dependencies>` will still take precedence (see S4).

#### ! Related
* [S3: Central Management (BOM)](../scenario-3-mgmt/guide.qmd) - The core mechanism used here.
* [S4: Direct Override](../scenario-4-override/guide.qmd) - Understanding the precedence of direct declarations.
* [S14: Rejecting a Version](../scenario-14-reject/guide.qmd) - Forcing *away* from a version.
